narrative:
Our Sunday is looking great with cool temperatures, a little breeze and plenty of sun. Clouds increase overnight and showers and thundershowers are scattered around Monday, mostly in the afternoon. More afternoon showers and thundershowers are scattered around Tuesday afternoon. Wednesday begins a dry trend for the end of the week. Temperatures remain cool through Wednesday before a warm up begins Thursday.

The June 2026 Forecast Outlook

The June 2026 Forecast Outlook is now available.

2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced

In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page .

RaysWeather.Com App

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Subscribe Saturday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion We end May on a fantastic note as high pressure provides beautiful weather. A cold front moving in from the north is the catalyst for showers and thundershowers to develop Monday, mostly in the afternoon. Temperatures remain cool. The cold front passes through Monday evening; clearing out the the skies. Unusually cold air aloft combined with the strong early June sun will allow for widely scattered showers and thundershowers to develop in the afternoon Tuesday. Temperatures are on the cool side. High pressure to the north is in firm control Wednesday through Saturday. We'll see dry conditions with plenty of sunshine and clear skies. Temperatures remain cool Wednesday with morning lows near 40 in the deeper valleys, but begin to warm Thursday through Saturday. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Wayne Presnell Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Spring time blooms. Submitted by Magnolia. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights June 2026 Forecast Outlook Monthly Forecast

June 2026 Forecast Outlook

Southern Appalachians – SW Virginia & Western North Carolina

The Typical June: Gentle Summer

With June 1, we enter “Meteorological Summer” even though the calendar still says “Spring.” The longest day of the year is the Summer Solstice, which occurs June 21 at 4:24 AM EDT this year. Temperatures continue to creep up, but average rainfall in the Southern Appalachians is similar to May overall.

Usually, a front or two can still move through from the northwest, giving us some variation in the weather, unlike later in the summer when a Bermuda High can block fronts and leave us in the warm muggies for weeks at a time. The potential for afternoon and evening thundershowers gradually creeps up in the second half of the month.

The Big Picture for June 2026

El Niño is building in the Pacific; however, its impact on our weather in June is rather limited. June 1 is the official beginning of hurricane season, which peaks in September. El Niño will have a limiting effect on hurricane development, which is why you are seeing forecasts of a less-than-average hurricane season. I caution that it only takes one to make it “above average” for a given location.

For several months running, a cold front has arrived around the end of the month or the first of the next — June is right on cue. That front ushers in a brief cooler period to start the month but pulls away quickly. High pressure builds in by midweek, dominating the pattern and bringing warmer weather through mid-month. A brief relaxation of that pattern brings a more variable week or so around mid-month, before high pressure reestablishes to close out June.

The Headline Graphic is from the ECMWF. It’s in the middle of the model guidance we analyzed, but we do think temperatures will be a little warmer than shown by that model.

Temperatures: Cool Start, Muddled Middle, Warm Finish

The first week of June has some refreshing cool mornings — enjoy them while they last. Warmer, more humid weather returns by the end of Week 1 and carries into Week 2. A front may stall near our region around mid-month, moderating temperatures for a few days. But that’s a temporary interruption — the rest of June trends warmer.

Precipitation: Early Deficit, Then Typical Summer Thundershowers

June starts with a couple of unsettled days, but the rest of Week 1 looks generally dry. The warm-up that follows may bring a period of wetter weather into mid-month, particularly if a front lingers nearby. After that, the pattern turns classic summer — heat-driven afternoon and evening thundershowers keeping precipitation near average through the back half of the month.

June Outlook at a Glance

Week Temperature Precipitation
Week 1 (June 1–7) Cool early but a warming trend late-week Drier than average
Week 2 (June 8–14) Slightly above average Slightly above average
Week 3 (June 15–21) Slightly above average Near average
Week 4 (June 22–30) Above average Near average
Overall for June Slightly above average Near average

Other Items of Note

  • El Niño is building in the Pacific. The graphic below shows the current Sea Surface Temperature Anomalies and the forecast for El Niño through early 2027. Note the warm water in the Equatorial Pacific — a clear sign of a developing El Niño. The right side of that graphic shows the probability forecast for El Niño into next winter. Not only is El Niño nearly a sure bet, but it is also expected to rival the strength of the 2023 event — or be the strongest since 2015. The event will likely continue into 2027, peaking in late fall or early winter.

Other than limiting Atlantic hurricane activity, El Niño has little correlation to weather in our region during the summer. But eventually, it will promote an active storm track across the Southern states, bringing stormy weather into the Southeast through fall and winter.

  • To produce this forecast, we used extended ECMWF, GFS, and Canadian modeling. They are in decent agreement overall. The GFS is the wettest of the three, and the Canadian is the warmest. They are not identical (they never are), but they do rhyme, which leads to a bit more confidence in this forecast than usual.
  • Our forecast is “from scratch,” based on large-scale modeling and climate factors. We do look at the NOAA forecast at the end to compare. For the first time since we started writing these monthly forecasts in September 2025, our forecast agrees with the NOAA forecast for June.
  • We believe much wetter weather is a good bet for June south and west of our region.

May 31, 2026 Monday Message for May 25, 2026 It's the Memorial Day Monday Message, where we recap our sorely-needed drought relief and discuss our region's cyclical warm/cool periods. May 25, 2026 Rainy, Cool Saturday It's a rainy, cool Saturday around western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia. High temperatures are running as much as 20-degrees below normal in the heart of the cold air damming zone. Sunday and Memorial Day will continue to be unsettled, but temperatures will moderate and there will be additional breaks in the clouds, as the rainfall pattern becomes more convective, or showery in nature. While the rainfall is falling on our holiday weekend, it's entirely beneficial with respect to our long-standing drought conditions. May 23, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
